next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

By the time Mira rolled LM-127B through the back hall of Blue Hour, the laundry had gone to its night noises. The customer bell was dark. One dryer still turned with a bad bearing whine, then knocked once every revolution like a loose tooth. Steam had settled into the hanging shirts and uniforms so the room felt upholstered in damp cloth. The body rode under a motel sheet on one of Jo's wire carts, ankles tapping the rail each time a wheel found the cracked grout by the sink.

She had signed the transfer under salvage review, which bought her until morning if nobody matched the hold shelf against the internal movement log. Morning would turn clerical lag into a list of things she could not afford: tampering, removal of company stock, falsification of disposal routing. She could have written the categories herself. Claims taught you the difference between a mistake and a billable act. It also taught you how quickly both became the same thing.

Tavi looked up from the wetbench when she came in. He had his tools laid out in weight order on a folded towel printed with lemons, because he treated every borrowed surface like it had to survive him. His long fingers were already gloved, but he still tapped each vial against the burn scar on his forearm before he opened it, a nervous little click-click that he would have denied if anyone named it. Steam had put a shine on the bridge of his flattened nose.

"Tell me again," he said, "why this one gets midnight bench time ahead of the people who are actually paying us."

Mira braked the cart with her knee. "Because if I le...
open_hooks_to_carry: Use Jo's laundry as a vivid social space rather than a neutral hideout.
Let the first batch of clients establish the range of overlap symptoms through action and repair work.
prev_tail_excerpt:
Mira held up her screen so she could see the cleared balance. There was no amount and no suspension banner, just the plain closed file.

Nidia stared at it. "That's it."

"That's it for now."

Nidia covered her mouth with both hands, braces and all. The sound that came out was not a sob exactly. It was smaller and stranger than that, like her body had forgotten what to do when something stopped hurting on time.

"I can work tomorrow," she said into her palms.

"Yes."

"Fuck."

Mira almost told her not to thank anybody. Instead she said, "Go home. Sleep if you can. If they call about anything, you tell them the file closed at local review and you ask for the note number. Nothing else."

Nidia nodded hard. Then, because people needed dignity even in alleys, she straightened and said, "The windows on fourteen are better after rain. You can see all the way to the ferry line."

Mira did not ask why she was telling her that now. She knew. It was the only gift Nidia had handy that wasn't cash.

"Good," Mira said. "Keep the sky."

Nidia left toward the tram with her tote bumping her hip and her bad wrists held close. Mira watched until she was gone, then looked down at the scrap of receipt paper in her own hand.

It was Sloan's number.

The terms were cash, cases, silence.

She folded it once and tucked it behind her work badge, where company policy said nothing personal belonged, then went back inside carrying the wetbench smell with her.
state_deltas_required: Mira and Tavi have almost no money, equipment, or cover, but the need for unofficial repairs appears immediately and faster than they can control.
Blue Hour's back room becomes a functioning illicit repair desk rather than a one-off favor.
